food_name: Cantonese Roast Duck with Rice (Siu Ngaap Fan)
aliases: Cantonese Roast Duck with Rice (Siu Ngaap Fan)

english: Cantonese Roast Duck with Rice (Siu Ngaap Fan)

region_of_origin: Guangdong (Canton), China; widely sold at siu mei roast meat stalls across Singapore
food_subgroup: Cantonese roast duck (crispy lacquered skin) served with steamed jasmine rice and dark aromatic gravy

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no dedicated concept for Cantonese Roast Duck with Rice. Closest parent is roasted whole duck with marinade (FOODON_03302050 — roasted duck product). No exact URI available; null used pending FoodOn expansion.
ontology.foodex2_description: Cantonese roast duck — whole duck marinated with five-spice and soy, air-dried and oven-roasted; served with steamed jasmine rice and dark aromatic gravy. FoodEx2 A03LC (Cooked meat dishes) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 leaf code exists for Cantonese roast duck rice as a composite dish. A03LC (Cooked meat dishes) is the closest defensible parent code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for Cantonese roast duck with rice as of April 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker plate of Cantonese roast duck with rice weighs approximately 400 g, comprising roughly 100 g of chopped roast duck (with skin), 200 g of steamed jasmine rice, and 100 g of dark aromatic gravy and plum sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Roast Duck with Rice (Cantonese) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Health Promotion Board Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Cantonese Roast Duck with Rice. This is the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values supplemented from USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, duck roasted + cooked jasmine rice composite) where HPB did not report these micronutrients; confidence 0.9 for those three fields.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Jasmine rice (GI ~73); University of Sydney GI Database entry for white jasmine rice
gi_source.source_match_notes: The GI of this composite dish is driven by the jasmine rice component (~200 g per 400 g plate, GI ~73 per University of Sydney GI Database). The duck fat and protein moderately attenuate the post-prandial glucose response but do not materially lower the overall GI of the plate. GL per 400 g serving = (73 × 82 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 59.9. Available carbohydrate calculated as 20.5 g/100 g × 400 g × 0.97 ≈ 82 g (subtracting fibre). This is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement of the composite dish exists in the University of Sydney database.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — 480 mg per 100 g; a standard 400 g plate delivers approximately 1920 mg sodium, approaching the entire WHO daily recommended limit of 2000 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Cantonese roast duck rice to occasional consumption, request the dark gravy on the side and use sparingly, and pair the dish with plain steamed vegetables without added oyster or soy sauce to reduce total meal sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load per full plate (GL ~59.9) driven by 200 g of jasmine rice (GI ~73); the duck protein and fat provide modest attenuation of the post-prandial glucose response but do not offset the large carbohydrate load.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should request a half-rice portion (approximately 100 g, reducing GL to ~30), avoid the plum sauce due to its high sugar content, limit the dark gravy, and prioritise the duck protein to support glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (2.8 g/100 g for the composite plate; duck skin alone is ~7–8 g/100 g saturated fat) combined with high sodium (480 mg/100 g) and cholesterol (58 mg/100 g) presents a cumulative cardiovascular risk in a full-plate serving.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should remove the duck skin before eating to substantially reduce saturated fat and cholesterol intake, limit the dark gravy to reduce sodium, and consume this dish no more than once per week as part of a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Five-spice marinade contains anti-inflammatory spices (star anise, cinnamon); selenium (22 mcg/100 g) and iron (1.5 mg/100 g) support immune and haematological function.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cantonese roast duck is generally suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the anti-inflammatory spice marinade and beneficial micronutrients (selenium, iron) are advantageous, though patients should remove the skin to limit saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cantonese Roast Duck with Rice (燒鴨飯, Siu Ngaap Fan) is one of Singapore's most iconic hawker dishes, rooted in the Cantonese siu mei (roast meat) tradition originating from Guangdong province, China. A whole duck is marinated with five-spice powder, star anise, cinnamon, soy sauce, galangal, and Chinese rice wine, then air-dried for several hours to tighten the skin. The duck is hung vertically in a high-heat commercial roasting oven and basted repeatedly with a maltose-soy glaze, producing the signature deep lacquered, mahogany-brown, glistening skin that is simultaneously crispy and flavourful. When served, the duck is chopped through the bone into bite-sized pieces and arranged over a mound of steamed jasmine rice, with a dark, aromatic gravy — made from the roasting juices, soy sauce, and spices — poured generously over the top. A small dish of plum sauce is typically served alongside for dipping. The composite dish (400 g plate: ~100 g duck, 200 g rice, 100 g gravy) provides approximately 780 kcal, 38 g protein, 35 g fat, and 82 g carbohydrate. Nutritionally, the dish is high in sodium (1920 mg per plate) and has a high glycemic load (~59.9) driven by the jasmine rice component (GI ~73). The duck meat is a good source of selenium (22 mcg/100 g), niacin (4.8 mg/100 g), zinc (1.8 mg/100 g), and vitamin B12 (0.5 mcg/100 g). Clinically, the dish is not recommended for patients with hypertension or type 2 diabetes without portion modification; removing the duck skin and requesting half rice significantly improves the nutritional profile. The five-spice marinade contributes anti-inflammatory compounds (cinnamaldehyde, anethole) that may be beneficial for inflammatory conditions.
common_user_queries: roast duck vs soy sauce chicken nutrition

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo, the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes. GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database (jasmine rice, GI 73) as the dominant glycemic driver of the composite dish. Three micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy at confidence 0.9. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content not reported by HPB for this composite dish and set to -1.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for the Cantonese roast duck with rice composite dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a composite entry as of April 2026. 2. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of April 2026. 3. Copper (0.2 mg/100 g), manganese (0.05 mg/100 g), and vitamin K (3.5 mcg/100 g) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (duck roasted + cooked jasmine rice weighted composite) where HPB did not report these micronutrients; confidence 0.9 for these three fields. 4. Portions: the primary serving is a standard hawker plate of 400 g (100 g duck + 200 g rice + 100 g gravy/sauce). A duplicate 250 g portion entry was present in the input and has been consolidated to a single 400 g default serving per HPB reference. 5. GI is an analogous estimate based on the jasmine rice component (GI ~73, University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement of the composite dish exists. GL per 400 g plate ≈ 59.9. 6. Halal status: Cantonese roast duck is traditionally prepared with Chinese rice wine and fermented soya beans; it is generally not halal-certified unless prepared at a certified halal establishment. is_halal set to false for the generic record.
